revision.text: '''Muraji''' (連). Muraji was an ancient [[Japan|Japanese]] hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a [[kabane]]) that was reserved for the most powerful clans among the [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] clans, and which rivaled the rank of [[omi]] in political power and standing. By tradition the muraji clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the Nakatomi (中臣), the Mononobe (物部) and the Inbe (忌部). When the kabane system was reformed into the [[eight kabane system]] in [[684]], the muraji had generally fallen out of favor, and it was dropped to seventh in rank out of eight.

'''Muraji''' (連). Muraji was an ancient [[Japan|Japanese]] hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a [[kabane]]) that was reserved for the most powerful among the [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The muraji rivaled the rank of [[omi]] in political power and standing during much of the [[Yamato period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether [[Buddhism]] should be accepted and issues of emperor succession. By tradition the muraji clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the Nakatomi (中臣), the Mononobe (物部) and the Inbe (忌部).
Like the omi, the most powerful among the muraji added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to muraji and was referred to as Ōmuraji (大連). Examples of Ōmuraji mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]] included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu of Japan|Emperor Richū]], Ōtomo no Muroya (大伴室屋), [[Otomo no Kanamura|Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), Mononobe no Me (物部目), Mononobe no Arakahi (物部麁鹿火), Mononobe no Okoshi (物部尾輿) and [[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the kabane system was reformed into the [[eight kabane system]] in [[684]], a few of the powerful muraji of the time were given the kabane of [[ason]], which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the kabane of [[sukune]], which ranked third. Muraji itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
[[ja:連]]

When the kabane system was reformed into the [[eight kabane]] system in [[684]], a few of the powerful muraji of the time were given the kabane of [[ason]], which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the kabane of [[sukune]], which ranked third. revision.text:
'''Muraji''' (連). ''Muraji'' was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[eight kabane|eight ''kabane'']] system in [[684]], a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
[[ja:連]]

'''Muraji''' (連). ''Muraji'' was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The ''muraji'' rivaled the rank of ''[[omi]]'' in political power and standing during much of the [[Yamato period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether [[Buddhism]] should be accepted and issues of emperor succession. By tradition the ''muraji'' clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the Nakatomi (中臣), the Mononobe (物部) and the Inbe (忌部).

When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[eight kabane|eight ''kabane'']] system in [[684]], a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.

Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Otomo no Kanamura|Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in [[684]], a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
{{Japan-hist-stub}}
[[Category:Ancient Japan]]
[[fr:Muraji]] [[ja:連]] [[uk:Мурадзі]]

Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''sukune'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikoji]] (物部伊コ弗), son of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikoji *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), son of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), died 536 *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), son of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
{{Portal|Ancient Japan}} {{Japan-hist-stub}}

{{Short description|Hereditary title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{nihongo|'''''Muraji'''''|連}} (from [[Old Japanese]]: ''muraⁿzi'' < *''mura-nusi'' "village master"<ref>{{cite book |editor=Alexander Vovin |editor2=William McClure|title=Studies in Japanese and Korean Historical Linguistics and Beyond|location=Leiden, Boston|publisher=Brill|year=2017|pages=80–89|chapter=On The Etymology of the Name of Mt. Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The ''muraji'' rivaled the rank of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' in political power and standing during much of the [[Kofun period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether [[Buddhism]] should be accepted and issues of imperial succession. By tradition the ''muraji'' clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] (中臣), the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] (物部) and the Inbe (忌部).
Like the ''omi'', the most powerful ''muraji'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''muraji'' and were referred to as ''Ōmuraji'' (大連). Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)

{{Japan-hist-stub}}

Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
==References== <references/> {{Portal|Ancient Japan}}

''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.

*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)

Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. By tradition, the ''muraji'' clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] (中臣), the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] (物部), and the Inbe (忌部).
Like the ''omi'', the most powerful ''muraji'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''muraji'' and were referred to as ''Ōmuraji'' (大連). Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
==References== <references/> {{Portal|Ancient Japan}}

{{Short description|Hereditary title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{nihongo|'''''Muraji'''''|連}} (from [[Old Japanese]]: ''muraⁿzi'' < *''mura-nusi'' "village master"<ref>{{cite book |editor=Alexander Vovin |editor2=William McClure|title=Studies in Japanese and Korean Historical Linguistics and Beyond|location=Leiden, Boston|publisher=Brill|year=2017|pages=80–89|chapter=On The Etymology of the Name of Mt. Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The ''muraji'' rivaled the rank of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' in political power and standing during much of the [[Kofun period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether [[Buddhism]] should be accepted and issues of imperial succession. By tradition, the ''muraji'' clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] (中臣), the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] (物部), and the Inbe (忌部).
Like the ''omi'', the most powerful ''muraji'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''muraji'' and were referred to as ''Ōmuraji'' (大連). Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
==References== <references/> {{Portal|Ancient Japan}}

When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
==References== <references/> {{Portal|Ancient Japan}}

''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.

{{Short description|Hereditary title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{nihongo|'''''Muraji'''''|連}} (from [[Old Japanese]]: ''muraⁿzi'' < *''mura-nusi'' "village master"<ref>{{cite book |editor=Alexander Vovin |editor2=William McClure|title=Studies in Japanese and Korean Historical Linguistics and Beyond|location=Leiden, Boston|publisher=Brill|year=2017|pages=80–89|chapter=On The Etymology of the Name of Mt. Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The ''muraji'' rivaled the rank of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' in political power and standing during much of the [[Kofun period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether [[Buddhism]] should be accepted and issues of imperial succession. By tradition, the ''muraji'' clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] (中臣), the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] (物部), and the Inbe (忌部).
Like the ''omi'', the most powerful ''muraji'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''muraji'' and were referred to as ''Ōmuraji'' (大連). Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
==References== <references/> {{Portal|Ancient Japan}}

{{Short description|Hereditary title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{nihongo|'''''Muraji'''''|連}} (from [[Old Japanese]]: ''muraⁿzi'' < *''mura-nusi'' "village master"<ref>{{cite book |editor=Alexander Vovin |editor2=William McClure|title=Studies in Japanese and Korean Historical Linguistics and Beyond|location=Leiden, Boston|publisher=Brill|year=2017|pages=80–89|chapter=On The Etymology of the Name of Mt. Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The ''muraji'' rivaled the rank of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' in political power and standing during much of the [[Kofun period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether [[Buddhism]] should be accepted and issues of imperial succession. By tradition, the ''muraji'' clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] (中臣), the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] (物部), and the Inbe (忌部).
Like the ''omi'', the most powerful ''muraji'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''muraji'' and were referred to as ''Ōmuraji'' (大連). Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
==References== <references/> {{Portal|Ancient Japan}}

{{Short description|Hereditary title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{nihongo|'''''Muraji'''''|連}} (from [[Old Japanese]]: ''muraⁿzi'' < *''mura-nusi'' "village master"<ref>{{cite book |editor=Alexander Vovin |editor2=William McClure|title=Studies in Japanese and Korean Historical Linguistics and Beyond|location=Leiden, Boston|publisher=Brill|year=2017|pages=80–89|chapter=On The Etymology of the Name of Mt. Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The ''muraji'' rivaled the rank of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' in political power and standing during much of the [[Kofun period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether [[Buddhism]] should be accepted and issues of imperial succession. By tradition, the ''muraji'' clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] (中臣), the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] (物部), and the Inbe (忌部).
Like the ''omi'', the most powerful ''muraji'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''muraji'' and were referred to as ''Ōmuraji'' (大連). Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
==References== <references/> {{Portal|Ancient Japan}}

Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The ''muraji'' rivaled the rank of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' in political power and standing during much of the [[Kofun period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether [[Buddhism]] should be accepted and issues of imperial succession. By tradition, the ''muraji'' clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] (中臣), the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] (物部), and the Inbe (忌部).
Like the ''omi'', the most powerful ''muraji'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''muraji'' and were referred to as ''Ōmuraji'' (大連). Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
==References== <references/> {{Portal|Ancient Japan}}

Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. ==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)

{{Short description|Hereditary title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{nihongo|'''''Muraji'''''|連}} (from [[Old Japanese]]: ''muraⁿzi'' < *''mura-nusi'' "village master"<ref>{{cite book |editor=Alexander Vovin |editor2=William McClure|title=Studies in Japanese and Korean Historical Linguistics and Beyond|location=Leiden, Boston|publisher=Brill|year=2017|pages=80–89|chapter=On The Etymology of the Name of Mt. Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The ''muraji'' rivaled the rank of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' in political power and standing during much of the [[Kofun period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether [[Buddhism]] should be accepted and issues of imperial succession. By tradition, the ''muraji'' clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] (中臣), the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] (物部), and the Inbe (忌部).
Like the ''omi'', the most powerful ''muraji'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''muraji'' and were referred to as ''Ōmuraji'' (大連). Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)

*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)

{{Short description|Hereditary title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{nihongo|'''''Muraji'''''|連}} (from [[Old Japanese]]: ''muraⁿzi'' < *''mura-nusi'' "village master"<ref>{{cite book |editor=Alexander Vovin |editor2=William McClure|title=Studies in Japanese and Korean Historical Linguistics and Beyond|location=Leiden, Boston|publisher=Brill|year=2017|pages=80–89|chapter=On The Etymology of the Name of Mt. Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The ''muraji'' rivaled the rank of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' in political power and standing during much of the [[Kofun period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether [[Buddhism]] should be accepted and issues of imperial succession. By tradition, the ''muraji'' clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] (中臣), the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] (物部), and the Inbe (忌部).
Like the ''omi'', the most powerful ''muraji'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''muraji'' and were referred to as ''Ōmuraji'' (大連). Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
==References== <references/> {{Portal|Ancient Japan}}

Like the ''omi'', the most powerful ''muraji'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''muraji'' and were referred to as ''Ōmuraji'' (大連). Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
==References== <references/> {{Portal|Ancient Japan}}
{{Japan-hist-stub}}

Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which Ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in Rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
==References== <references/> {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} {{Japan-hist-stub}}

Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting Rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The ''muraji'' rivaled the Rank of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' in political power and standing during much of the [[Kofun period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether [[Buddhism]] should be accepted and issues of imperial succession. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in Rank.

*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)

revision.comment: Bot: Add Wikidata has short description - based on Wikidata English short description
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Muraji]][[es:Muraji]][[ff:Muraji]][[fr:Muraji]][[it:Muraji]][[ja:連]][[ko:무라지]][[pt:Muraji]][[ru:Мурадзи]][[uk:Мурадзі]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2735611]] Wikidata name: Muraji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Hereditary title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{nihongo|'''''Muraji'''''|連}} (from [[:en:Old Japanese|Old Japanese]]: ''muraⁿzi'' < *''mura-nusi'' "village master"<ref>{{cite book |editor=Alexander Vovin |editor2=William McClure|title=Studies in Japanese and Korean Historical Linguistics and Beyond|location=Leiden, Boston|publisher=Brill|year=2017|pages=80–89|chapter=On The Etymology of the Name of Mt. Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting Rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The ''muraji'' rivaled the Rank of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' in political power and standing during much of the [[Kofun period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] should be accepted and issues of imperial succession. By tradition, the ''muraji'' clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] (中臣), the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] (物部), and the Inbe (忌部).
Like the ''omi'', the most powerful ''muraji'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''muraji'' and were referred to as ''Ōmuraji'' (大連). Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[:en:Mononobe no Arakabi|Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which Ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in Rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[:en:Mononobe no Arakabi|Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
==References== <references/> {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} {{Japan-hist-stub}}

When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which Ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in Rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[:en:Mononobe no Arakabi|Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)

Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting Rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The ''muraji'' rivaled the Rank of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' in political power and standing during much of the [[Kofun period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] should be accepted and issues of imperial succession. By tradition, the ''muraji'' clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] (中臣), the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] (物部), and the Inbe (忌部).
Like the ''omi'', the most powerful ''muraji'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''muraji'' and were referred to as ''Ōmuraji'' (大連). Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[:en:Mononobe no Arakabi|Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which Ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in Rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[:en:Mononobe no Arakabi|Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Muraji]][[es:Muraji]][[ff:Muraji]][[fr:Muraji]][[it:Muraji]][[ja:連]][[ko:무라지]][[pt:Muraji]][[ru:Мурадзи]][[uk:Мурадзі]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Muraji]][[es:Muraji]][[ff:Muraji]][[fr:Muraji]][[it:Muraji]][[ja:連]][[ko:무라지]][[pt:Muraji]][[ru:Мурадзи]][[uk:Мурадзі]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Muraji]][[es:Muraji]][[ff:Muraji]][[fr:Muraji]][[it:Muraji]][[ja:連]][[ko:무라지]][[pt:Muraji]][[ru:Мурадзи]][[uk:Мурадзі]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2735611]] Wikidata name: Muraji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Hereditary title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{nihongo|'''''Muraji'''''|連}} (from [[:en:Old Japanese|Old Japanese]]: ''muraⁿzi'' < *''mura-nusi'' "village master"<ref>{{cite book |editor=Alexander Vovin |editor2=William McClure|title=Studies in Japanese and Korean Historical Linguistics and Beyond|location=Leiden, Boston|publisher=Brill|year=2017|pages=80–89|chapter=On The Etymology of the Name of Mt. Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting Rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The ''muraji'' rivaled the Rank of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' in political power and standing during much of the [[Kofun period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] should be accepted and issues of imperial succession. By tradition, the ''muraji'' clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] (中臣), the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] (物部), and the Inbe (忌部).
Like the ''omi'', the most powerful ''muraji'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''muraji'' and were referred to as ''Ōmuraji'' (大連). Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), [[:en:Mononobe no Arakabi|Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部麁鹿火), [[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部尾輿) and [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which Ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in Rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[:en:Mononobe no Arakabi|Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
==References== <references/> {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} {{Japan-hist-stub}}

''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in Rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *[[:en:Mononobe no Arakabi|Mononobe no Arakabi]] (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *[[:en:Mononobe no Okoshi|Mononobe no Okoshi]] (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *[[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)

*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *{{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|en|Mononobe no Arakabi|WD=Q1114837|lt=Mononobe no Arakabi}} (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *{{ill|Mononobe no Okoshi|en|Mononobe no Okoshi|WD=Q11570693|lt=Mononobe no Okoshi}} (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *{{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|WD=Q2424736|lt=Mononobe no Moriya}} (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Muraji]][[es:Muraji]][[ff:Muraji]][[fr:Muraji]][[it:Muraji]][[ja:連]][[ko:무라지]][[pt:Muraji]][[ru:Мурадзи]][[uk:Мурадзі]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Muraji]][[es:Muraji]][[ff:Muraji]][[fr:Muraji]][[it:Muraji]][[ja:連]][[ko:무라지]][[pt:Muraji]][[ru:Мурадзи]][[uk:Мурадзі]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Muraji]][[es:Muraji]][[ff:Muraji]][[fr:Muraji]][[it:Muraji]][[ja:連]][[ko:무라지]][[pt:Muraji]][[ru:Мурадзи]][[uk:Мурадзі]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2735611]] Wikidata name: Muraji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Hereditary title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{nihongo|'''''Muraji'''''|連}} (from {{ill|Old Japanese|en|Old Japanese|WD=Q5736700|lt=Old Japanese}}: ''muraⁿzi'' < *''mura-nusi'' "village master"<ref>{{cite book |editor=Alexander Vovin |editor2=William McClure|title=Studies in Japanese and Korean Historical Linguistics and Beyond|location=Leiden, Boston|publisher=Brill|year=2017|pages=80–89|chapter=On The Etymology of the Name of Mt. Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}ese hereditary title denoting Rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The ''muraji'' rivaled the Rank of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' in political power and standing during much of the [[Kofun period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} should be accepted and issues of imperial succession. By tradition, the ''muraji'' clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] (中臣), the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] (物部), and the Inbe (忌部).
Like the ''omi'', the most powerful ''muraji'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''muraji'' and were referred to as ''Ōmuraji'' (大連). Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|en|Mononobe no Arakabi|WD=Q1114837|lt=Mononobe no Arakabi}} (物部麁鹿火), {{ill|Mononobe no Okoshi|en|Mononobe no Okoshi|WD=Q11570693|lt=Mononobe no Okoshi}} (物部尾輿) and {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|WD=Q2424736|lt=Mononobe no Moriya}} (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which Ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in Rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *{{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|en|Mononobe no Arakabi|WD=Q1114837|lt=Mononobe no Arakabi}} (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *{{ill|Mononobe no Okoshi|en|Mononobe no Okoshi|WD=Q11570693|lt=Mononobe no Okoshi}} (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *{{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|WD=Q2424736|lt=Mononobe no Moriya}} (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
==References== <references/> {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} {{Japan-hist-stub}}

Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|en|Mononobe no Arakabi|WD=Q1114837|lt=Mononobe no Arakabi|1=Mononobe no Arakabi}} (物部麁鹿火), {{ill|Mononobe no Okoshi|en|Mononobe no Okoshi|WD=Q11570693|lt=Mononobe no Okoshi|1=Mononobe no Okoshi}} (物部尾輿) and {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|WD=Q2424736|lt=Mononobe no Moriya|1=Mononobe no Moriya}} (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which Ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in Rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *{{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|en|Mononobe no Arakabi|WD=Q1114837|lt=Mononobe no Arakabi|1=Mononobe no Arakabi}} (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *{{ill|Mononobe no Okoshi|en|Mononobe no Okoshi|WD=Q11570693|lt=Mononobe no Okoshi|1=Mononobe no Okoshi}} (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *{{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|WD=Q2424736|lt=Mononobe no Moriya|1=Mononobe no Moriya}} (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)

{{Short description|Hereditary title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{nihongo|'''''Muraji'''''|連}} (from {{ill|Old Japanese|en|Old Japanese|qid=Q5736700|lt=Old Japanese|1=Old Japanese}}: ''muraⁿzi'' < *''mura-nusi'' "village master"<ref>{{cite book |editor=Alexander Vovin |editor2=William McClure|title=Studies in Japanese and Korean Historical Linguistics and Beyond|location=Leiden, Boston|publisher=Brill|year=2017|pages=80–89|chapter=On The Etymology of the Name of Mt. Fuji|url=https://www.academia.edu/35892155|isbn=978-90-04-35085-4}}</ref>) was an ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}ese hereditary title denoting Rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that was reserved for the most powerful among the ''Tomo no [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Miyatsuko]]'' clans, which were clans associated with particular occupations. The ''muraji'' rivaled the Rank of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' in political power and standing during much of the [[Kofun period]] and were frequently in conflict with them over political issues such as whether {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} should be accepted and issues of imperial succession. By tradition, the ''muraji'' clans claimed descent from mythological gods (神別氏族, ''shinbetsu shizoku'') and included such clans as the Ōtomo (大伴), the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] (中臣), the [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] (物部), and the Inbe (忌部).
Like the ''omi'', the most powerful ''muraji'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''muraji'' and were referred to as ''Ōmuraji'' (大連). Examples of ''Ōmuraji'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Mononobe no Ikofutsu (物部伊莒弗) during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], [[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋), [[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), [[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|en|Mononobe no Arakabi|qid=Q1114837|lt=Mononobe no Arakabi|1=Mononobe no Arakabi}} (物部麁鹿火), {{ill|Mononobe no Okoshi|en|Mononobe no Okoshi|qid=Q11570693|lt=Mononobe no Okoshi|1=Mononobe no Okoshi}} (物部尾輿) and {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|qid=Q2424736|lt=Mononobe no Moriya|1=Mononobe no Moriya}} (物部守屋).
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the eight ''[[kabane]]'' system in 684, a few of the powerful ''muraji'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, but most were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[sukune]]'', which Ranked third. ''Muraji'' itself was dropped to seventh in Rank.
==List of ''Ō-muraji'' (大連)==
*[[Mononobe no Toochone]] (物部十千根) *[[Mononobe no Ikofutsu]] (物部伊莒弗), great-grandson of Toochone *[[Ōtomo no Muroya]] (大伴室屋) *[[Mononobe no Me]] (物部目), son of Ikofutsu *[[Ōtomo no Kanamura]] (大伴金村), grandson of Muroya *[[Mononobe no Itabi]] (物部木蓮子), grandson of Ikofutsu *{{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|en|Mononobe no Arakabi|qid=Q1114837|lt=Mononobe no Arakabi|1=Mononobe no Arakabi}} (物部 麁鹿火), grandson of Itabi (died 536) *{{ill|Mononobe no Okoshi|en|Mononobe no Okoshi|qid=Q11570693|lt=Mononobe no Okoshi|1=Mononobe no Okoshi}} (物部 尾輿), grandson of Me *[[Mononobe no Nieko]] (物部 贄子), son of Okoshi *{{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|qid=Q2424736|lt=Mononobe no Moriya|1=Mononobe no Moriya}} (物部 守屋), son of Okoshi (died 587)
